title: Mother's Day high tea brings connection for women who have experienced pregnancy and infant loss
content:
A bereaved mother is offered support at Frances Perry House maternity hospital in Melbourne. ( Supplied: Eliza Strauss, Frances Perry House )
Women who have experienced pregnancy loss are breaking the silence at an annual Mother's Day high tea.
About 2,300 babies each year do not survive childbirth, and the event run by charity Bears of Hope aims to give mums a safe space to discuss and grieve their loss.
Mothers who participate in the event say it has helped them remove the stigma around discussing infant loss.
Jessica Rogers will never forget the day she had to leave hospital after giving birth, without her baby in her arms.
Warning: This content contains information and images relating to pregnancy loss and neonatal loss, which some readers might find distressing.
Her daughter Willow was stillborn at 24 weeks, after developing a tumour on her lung that was pressing on her heart.
"During the actual moment, it was all such a blur because so many emotions are going through your mind … just devastation," Ms Rogers said.
"We had about 24 hours … once we had birthed her, we got to hold her, and name her … and had time with our family so we could all grieve.
Jessica Rogers holding her stillborn baby Willow. ( Supplied: Jessica Rogers )
That was in 2017, and Ms Rogers's eldest child was four at the time.
Nearly a decade later, Ms Rogers is now a mother of three and said talking about Willow had been an important part of processing her grief.
Jessica Rogers with her husband and three sons, taking part in a Bears of Hope event. ( Supplied: Jessica Rogers )
She is looking forward to sharing stories with others at an upcoming Mother's Day high tea run by the charity Bears of Hope for women who have experienced pregnancy and infant loss.
Ms Rogers is attending the event in Laurieton in northern New South Wales, which is one of 18 Bears of Hope high teas being held across Australia on May 9, the day before Mother's Day.
summary: A bereaved mother is offered support at Frances Perry House maternity hospital in Melbourne. ( Supplied: Eliza Strauss, Frances Perry House )
author: Emma Siossian

title: Shock after baby dies, another hospitalised after birth in Wagga Wagga homeless camp
content:
Police were called to a site along the river downstream from Wagga Beach in Wagga Wagga. ( ABC News: Lauren Pezet )
A baby has died and another is in hospital after a 37-year-old woman gave birth at a homeless camp on the weekend.
The Wagga Women's Health Centre said it was a "terrible day" for the community and hoped it marks a "tipping point" for action on the homelessness crisis.
Police believe there were no suspicious circumstances. A report will be prepared for the coroner.
Women's health advocates say the death of a baby during birth at a homeless camp in southern New South Wales should be a "tipping point" for action on the growing housing crisis.
Emergency services were called to the site along the Murrumbidgee River near Wagga Beach on Saturday afternoon following a concern for welfare.
"Officers from Riverina Police District attended and located a 37-year-old woman, along with two infants; however, one was deceased," a NSW Police spokesperson said in a statement.
Police believe there are no suspicious circumstances and a report is being prepared for the coroner.
The ABC understands the babies had just been born along an area of the river where there are currently about a dozen tents set up long-term.
Piles of rubbish can be seen surrounding some camps, as well as prams, children's toys and bikes.
The Murrumbidgee Local Health District declined to comment while the incident was being investigated by NSW Police.
NSW Minister for Homelessness Rose Jackson said in a statement that the news was "heartbreaking" and confirmed the family was known to Homes NSW.
"A lot is still unknown about this tragic event and as such I've asked Homes NSW for a full investigation into the family's circumstances," she said
Wagga Women's Health Centre president Vickie Burkinshaw said it was a "terrible day for our community" that a woman would be in that situation.
The centre currently has a waitlist of 76 women needing help.
summary: Police were called to a site along the river downstream from Wagga Beach in Wagga Wagga. ( ABC News: Lauren Pezet )
author: Lauren Pezet

title: Australian women have overtaken men in prescribing rates for ADHD medication
content:
More women are being prescribed medication for ADHD compared to men in Australia. ( Four Corners: Patrick Forrest )
Amber Davis speaks through tears as she describes the overwhelming emotion of being diagnosed with ADHD as an adult.
"Finding out when you're in your fifties is completely heartbreaking because you have a massive grief around all that you've lost. I can't tell you how incredibly difficult life is, and how you never get that back."
When she first started treatment she felt a great sense of relief and now feels like she can finally achieve her goals.
"Before I got treatment, life was absolute hell. Trying to fit in, trying to do things the right way when you've got a brain so full, so busy, so many goals that you want to achieve, but you can't do most of the things that [neurotypical] people do."
It's only in hindsight that Celine Wanjiku realises there were signs of ADHD in her childhood.
"It was pretty invisible when I was a child," she said.
"Apart from the odd not finishing assignments here and there, daydreaming in class."
"I was just told a lot: 'Apply yourself a bit more. You need to try harder.'"
Getting diagnosed in her 20s was life-changing.
Across Australia, the rate of ADHD diagnoses — based on the percentage of adults filling scripts for ADHD medicine — has risen by almost 600 per cent since 2017 .
But within that sharp increase lies an unexpected story about women.
That trend is very clear when you look at the chart below.
Until late 2022, it was more common for men to fill a prescription for ADHD medication. This could be partly explained because of a male bias in the past leading to an under-recognition in women.
Now, the filled prescription rates have flipped, with women noticeably ahead of men.
summary: More women are being prescribed medication for ADHD compared to men in Australia. ( Four Corners: Patrick Forrest )
author: Norman Swan

title: Do acupuncture and herbs actually help with menopause symptoms?
content:
Acupuncture comes in several forms and you can have it with and without other therapies. ( Unsplash: Antonika Chanel )
Hot flushes, night sweats or swinging mood changes are some of the most common symptoms of menopause — the stage of a woman's life when menstrual periods stop permanently, and she is no longer fertile.
Some women choose to ride out the symptoms. Some choose hormone replacement therapy (HRT), also known as menopausal hormone therapy or MHT. This contains oestrogen, progesterone or combined therapies. Others use complementary therapies.
But do complementary therapies such as acupuncture and herbal medicines actually help?
Menopause is a normal part of aging, as is the menopausal transition (or perimenopause), which occurs for several years before it. Some women's periods stop earlier than others. But most women become menopausal naturally between the ages of 45 and 55 .
During menopause, women often have a range of symptoms . These can include hot flushes, night sweats, mood swings, joint discomfort, sleep disturbances, decreased libido, headache or migraine, cardiometabolic disturbances (such as high blood pressure), weight gain, and loss of bone mineral density.
These symptoms can be distressing and can affect women's quality of life.
Some women prefer to use complementary therapies alongside conventional treatment, or instead of it, due to side effects of menopausal hormone therapy.
Other women cannot use MHT because of other medical conditions, such as breast cancer, or being at risk of venous thromboembolism (blood clots in the deep veins of the leg, which can be fatal if they travel to the lungs).
But what does the evidence say about complementary therapies used in menopause?
'Brain fog' during perimenopause is real, and it can disrupt women's lives and careers.
Earlier this year, we and our colleagues published a large review to draw together the evidence. We analysed 158 clinical trials and systematic reviews conducted in women over 40. These studies looked at 86 complementary therapies, such as acupuncture, Chinese herbal medicine, vitamin and nutrient supplements, and mind-body approaches.
Most studies were of low or very low quality. This could be because they included a small number of participants, were not double-blinded (when neither the participants nor the researchers knew which people were given which therapy) and sometimes did not use placebos.
So clinicians don't have sufficient evidence to recommend them.
Most studies in the review asked women to report the frequency and severity of their symptoms. Some used questionnaires covering a range of symptoms to give an overall menopause score. Others just asked about hot flushes.
summary: Acupuncture comes in several forms and you can have it with and without other therapies. ( Unsplash: Antonika Chanel )
author: Evangeline Mantzioris, Alison Maunder and Carolyn Ee

title: Woman claims 'toxic dose' of wrong drug during routine surgery at PRH left her brain damaged
content:
Kimberley Gleed says she suffered a brain injury after she was given medication "in error" during routine surgery in Darwin. ( ABC News: Jayden O'Neill )
Kimberley Gleed is suing the Northern Territory government, claiming a doctor at Palmerston Regional Hospital gave her a "toxic dose" of the wrong drug.
The government has admitted the drug was administered "in error" but denies the amount the 36-year-old was given was a "toxic dose".
Ms Gleed is seeking compensation for medical expenses, ongoing care and loss of earnings, with the case returning to court next month.
A Top End woman who claims she suffered a hypoxic brain injury after a doctor mistakenly gave her a "toxic dose" of the wrong medication ahead of routine surgery is suing the Northern Territory government for negligence.
In documents filed with the Supreme Court, Kimberley Gleed says she was admitted to Palmerston Regional Hospital (PRH) in March 2022 for surgery to remove a benign tumour from her wrist.
But the 36-year-old claims she was rushed to the emergency room and placed in an induced coma after a doctor administered the drug "in error" while she was being anaesthetised.
The NT government has admitted the doctor's error, but disputes that the amount of drug administered amounted to a "toxic dose". ( ABC News: Jayden O'Neill )
Ms Gleed claims an unnamed doctor at the hospital had meant to administer the anti-nausea drug ondansetron but instead gave her 10 milligrams of metaraminol, used to treat hypotension.
Mia Pilbrow is taking legal action against a Northern Territory hospital and calling for the territory to adopt tailored pre-term labour guidelines, after medical staff sent her home seven times during her pregnancy, repeatedly assuring her the baby was fine.
Ms Gleed claims she was not hypotensive at the time and metaraminol should only be administered "in small incremental doses" at a maximum of 1mg at a time, with 10mg amounting to "a toxic dose".
"The doctor had intended to administer the plaintiff intravenous ondansetron and not metaraminol," the document reads.
As a result of the mistake, Ms Gleed claims her heart rate fell to about 25 beats per minute and her blood pressure spiked before she "developed acute pulmonary oedema".
Ms Gleed says she was then rushed to the intensive care unit (ICU) at Royal Darwin Hospital where she was diagnosed with "severe left ventricular dysfunction in the context of high dose metaraminol".
Two days later, Ms Gleed says she was discharged from the ICU but remained an outpatient for a further two days.
summary: Kimberley Gleed says she suffered a brain injury after she was given medication "in error" during routine surgery in Darwin. ( ABC News: Jayden O'Neill )
author: Jason Walls

title: Coroner considers if partner contributed to man's death after life support was switched off
content:
Strathbogie man Matthew Hamill died from meningitis-related symptoms after being discharged from Wangaratta Hospital. ( ABC News )
The Coroner's Court of Victoria is investigating after a woman confessed to intervening in the death of her partner, Strathbogie man Matthew Hamill, while he was a patient at Royal Melbourne Hospital in 2021.
The court was told she covered his face with her hand when his life support was switched off, to ensure his organs could be harvested in time.
Coroner Catherine Fitzgerald has reserved her judgement in the matter for a later date.
The Coroner's Court of Victoria is investigating whether the partner of a Royal Melbourne Hospital patient attempted to hasten his death in the moments after his life support was switched off.
In late 2020, 49-year-old Strathbogie man Matthew Hamill presented to the Wangaratta Hospital with cold symptoms, a headache, dizziness and nausea but was sent home after an assessment by a senior doctor.
The next morning, his family found him unresponsive and he was flown to The Royal Melbourne Hospital.
Yesterday, the Victorian Coroner's Court heard Mr Hamill had suffered significant brain damage from a stroke after contracting meningitis.
The inquest focused on the nearly two-week period he was at The Royal Melbourne Hospital and the actions of his partner.
The partner, Rebecca Day, was the only person present on January 16, 2021 after Mr Hamill's life support was turned off and he died.
The court heard that some years later, she confessed to intervening in his death, prompting a homicide investigation.
Taking the witness stand during the inquest, Detective Leading Constable Sergeant Kate Lynch said she arrested Ms Day in November 2023 over Mr Hamill's death after she had given a statement to police.
Detectives considered whether to lay charges against the woman after she admitted placing a hand over Mr Hamill's nose about 25 minutes after the life support was turned off.
She told police in her confession that the family had agreed to donate Mr Hamill's organs, but she believed they would not be viable more than 30 minutes after life support was turned off.
The court heard she despaired that the family would not get their wish and sought to intervene on their behalf.
summary: Strathbogie man Matthew Hamill died from meningitis-related symptoms after being discharged from Wangaratta Hospital. ( ABC News )
author: Andrew Mangelsdorf

title: When is the best time to get your flu shot? Two infectious diseases experts explain
content:
Each year the flu vaccine is designed to protect against a mix of different virus strains, depending on which ones are circulating. ( Unsplash, CDC )
We usually have to wait until winter approaches before we see an increase in cases of influenza, or the flu. But we have already seen a lot of flu this year, with 25,000 cases reported from January to March — and that's only a fraction of actual case numbers.
Most people with the flu recover without treatment. But it can cause serious complications in older people, young children, pregnant women and those with underlying chronic diseases such as asthma or heart disease. Influenza kills around 3,500 Australians a year and lands 18,000 in hospital.
There were more deaths associated with influenza last year than any other year this century, according to the Australia Bureau of Statistics.
Vaccination is the best way to protect against flu and is recommended for everyone over the age of six months.
Flu vaccines are free for certain risk groups . Others can access them (usually for a fee) at pharmacies, GP clinics and local council clinics in some states and territories. Some employers also offer vaccinations for staff.
Influenza has been unpredictable since the COVID pandemic, with off-season circulation. There are also concerns that protection may lag at the end of winter. So when is the best time to get vaccinated?
Flu symptoms include a cough, sore throat, fever, body aches and fatigue.
Flu spreads from person to person via small respiratory droplets when you talk, cough and sneeze. It may also spread by touching a surface or object where infected droplets have landed, but this is less common.
So if you have flu-like symptoms , it's important to stay at home.
Flu spreads more in the winter months due to increased contact between people and time spent indoors. Some studies also suggest influenza viruses transmit better in the cold .
Typically, human flu cases are caused by four virus strains, A(H3N2), A(H1N1), B/Victoria, and B/Yamagata.
So far this year in Australia , almost 98 per cent of cases have been influenza A(H3N2), and the remaining have been influenza B/Victoria.
Theoretically, the best time to get the flu vaccine is about two weeks before flu cases start to rise. ( ABC News: Kym Agius )
In late 2025 , a new variant of the H3N2 strain known as subclade K or "super-K" emerged in the Northern Hemisphere winter.
summary: Each year the flu vaccine is designed to protect against a mix of different virus strains, depending on which ones are circulating. ( Unsplash, CDC )
author: Meru Sheel and Allen Cheng
